author: y4rr
content:
Landed.

## What landed in \`ferrotorch-ml/src/metrics.rs\`

### 5 new probability-scoring metrics
- \`brier_score_loss(y_true, y_prob)\` — binary, mean squared error of predicted probabilities (1-D)
- \`d2_brier_score(y_true, y_prob)\` — D² skill score: 1 - (model_brier / null_brier)
- \`top_k_accuracy_score(y_true, y_score, k)\` — multi-class, takes \`[N, n_classes]\` 2-D scores
- \`zero_one_loss(y_true, y_pred, normalize)\` — fraction (\`true\`) or count (\`false\`)
- \`average_precision_score(y_true, y_score)\` — area under PR curve, binary

### Note on \`log_loss\`
The 2-D \`log_loss\` wrapper had already landed in #598 — its signature already takes a \`[N, K]\` 2-D probability tensor and matches \`sklearn.metrics.log_loss\`. Mentioning here for completeness; nothing new to add.

## Bonus: pre-existing flaky test fixed
While running workspace tests during this issue I caught \`test_cuda_rng_fork_join\` racing against \`test_cuda_rng_next_seed\` — both touch the process-global \`cuda_rng::RNG_STATE\` mutex (a single \`Mutex<u64>\` in \`ferrotorch_core::quantize\`). Under cargo's default parallel runner, one test's fork/join could observe state mutated by the other.

Fix: added a static test-local lock (same pattern as \`capture_lock\` in \`test_gpu_graph_pool.rs\` from #602) that both tests acquire. Verified stable across 3 sequential workspace runs.

## Tests (9 new)
- \`brier_score_perfect_predictions_is_zero\` — perfect ⇒ 0
- \`brier_score_uniform_half_loss\` — closed-form check (mean of 0.25 across two samples)
- \`d2_brier_perfect_score_is_one\`
- \`top_k_accuracy_k_equals_classes_is_one\` — k = num_classes ⇒ always correct
- \`top_k_accuracy_k_one_matches_argmax\` — k=1 case is standard accuracy
- \`top_k_rejects_1d_y_score\` — shape error path
- \`zero_one_loss_normalize_matches_hamming\` — fraction case = hamming_loss
- \`zero_one_loss_unnormalized_count\` — count case
- \`average_precision_perfect_separation\` — clean class boundary ⇒ AP = 1

47 ml metrics tests pass total. Workspace tests: zero regressions across 3 sequential runs.
